Name: spectral_quantization_routing
Title: Spectral Quantization Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Activation Covariance Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free precision allocation and real-time activation geometry-based routing.
Related Work: The proposed idea builds upon and differs from existing works such as Spectral Latent Steering (SLS) and Geometric Signal Separation, which focus on test-time optimization and in-context learning robustness. Our approach, Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), uniquely integrates activation covariance geometry with mixed-precision inference, providing a transparent and reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Abstract: This paper proposes Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, SQR maps these diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds, linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SQR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate SQR on WILDS benchmarks, comparing its throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiment 2: Investigate the impact of activation covariance geometry on quantization sensitivity, analyzing the relationship between local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates.
Experiment 3: Assess the effectiveness of SQR in enforcing strict latency budgets, measuring the reduction in latency compared to full-precision and static mixed-precision inference.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may require careful tuning of hyperparameters, such as the threshold for precision switching and the number of principal components to retain.
SQR may not be effective in scenarios where the activation geometry is highly non-stationary or where the input distribution shifts significantly.
The theoretical guarantees provided by PAC-Bayes bounds may not hold in practice, and further empirical evaluation is necessary to validate the approach.
